content.text: Reliability and robustness are the central obstacle between impressive agent demos and dependable production systems. An agent that succeeds nine times out of ten looks remarkable in a demo and is often unusable in practice, because multi-step tasks compound per-step error rates into low end-to-end success.

Reliability is the property that an agent produces correct results consistently across runs and inputs; robustness is the property that it degrades gracefully — rather than catastrophically — when inputs are noisy, adversarial, or out of distribution. Together they describe whether an agent can be trusted to act without a human watching every step.

The problem is distinctive to agents because they act over long horizons. A chatbot's single wrong answer is a local error; an agent's single wrong tool call can corrupt every subsequent step. Reliability engineering for agents is therefore as much about containment and recovery as about raw model accuracy.

The economic value of agents lies in unsupervised operation. If every action needs human review, the agent has saved no labour. Reliability is what makes autonomy worth deploying: it is the difference between a tool that drafts a suggestion and one that can be trusted to complete a task end to end.
. If each step succeeds with probability
. At 95% per-step reliability, a twenty-step task succeeds only about a third of the time — which is why per-step improvements that look marginal produce large end-to-end gains.
— how multi-step execution accumulates state and error.
— measuring success rates and distinguishing systematic from random failure.
— safely repeating operations without duplicating effects.
— why performance drops on inputs unlike the training or test distribution.

The single most important reliability fact about agents is that errors multiply over steps. Reducing the number of steps, or catching and correcting errors mid-trajectory, is often more effective than marginally improving the base model.

Sampling-based generation means the same input can yield different trajectories. This makes failures hard to reproduce and means a single successful run is weak evidence; reliability must be measured over many runs, not observed once.

Agents are brittle at the edges of their training distribution — unusual phrasings, rare tools, or adversarial inputs. Robustness is the capacity to fail softly there: to recognise uncertainty and defer or ask, rather than confidently do the wrong thing.

The worst failures are silent: an agent that misreads a value and proceeds confidently produces a plausible but wrong result with no error raised. Detecting silent failures requires verification, not just exception handling.

attrs.alt: Reliability control loop wrapping each agent step with verify and recover

content.text: The architectural response to unreliability is to wrap each risky step in a verify-and-recover envelope, so an error is caught where it happens rather than propagating. This trades extra calls for a far higher end-to-end success rate.

Robustness engineering combines several mechanisms: verification of intermediate results, retries with backoff for transient failures, idempotency so retries are safe, and escalation to a human when confidence is low. The expected end-to-end reliability with per-step recovery improves because a recoverable error no longer terminates the task.
is the probability that the recovery mechanism rescues a failed step. Even a modest
sharply raises effective per-step reliability, and because success compounds, the end-to-end gain is amplified.
A robust step wrapper verifies output, retries transient errors idempotently, and escalates when it cannot recover.

Picture a relay race where each runner drops the baton one time in twenty. With one runner, the team almost always finishes. With twenty runners, a dropped baton somewhere is nearly certain, and the race ends the moment the baton hits the ground. Agent reliability is that relay: the fix is not only faster runners but hand-offs where a dropped baton can be picked up. Verification and recovery are the rules that let the team continue after a fumble instead of being disqualified.
tasks are long-horizon and unsupervised — every added step multiplies the payoff of per-step reliability.
silent failures are costly, such as data modification or financial actions.
steps have side effects that must not be duplicated by a retry.
an error is fatal (bad auth, malformed request) — retrying wastes budget.
tasks are single-step and cheap to redo; the overhead is not worth it.

Autonomous coding agents illustrate reliability engineering at scale. Such an agent edits a repository over dozens of steps; a single wrong edit can break the build. Production systems make progress checkpoint-able (commit after each verified change), verify with the test suite after every edit, and roll back to the last green state on failure rather than pressing on. The test suite is the external verifier that makes autonomy safe.

def apply_change_safely(repo, change, run_tests):
    repo.checkpoint()                      # snapshot to enable rollback
    repo.apply(change)
    result = run_tests()
    if result.passed:
        repo.commit(f"verified: {change.summary}")
        return "committed"
    repo.rollback()                        # return to last known-good state
    return f"reverted: {result.failing_tests}"

Verification and retries cost extra calls, so reliability trades latency and money for success rate. The trade is usually favourable because a failed long task wastes all the compute already spent on it. The key metric is not per-step accuracy but end-to-end success rate at fixed cost; systems tune verification depth per step according to how expensive a silent failure would be.
— LangSmith, Langfuse, and OpenTelemetry capture every step for reproduction and failure analysis.
— run many trajectories to estimate success-rate distributions, not single runs.
— Temporal and similar engines provide retries, checkpoints, and exactly-once semantics.
— output validators and schema enforcers that turn silent errors into caught ones.
Robustness overlaps with security: an adversary who can push the agent out of distribution can trigger failures on demand. Defend the boundaries.
— validate and sanitise inputs; assume some are crafted to induce failure.
— on uncertainty about a high-impact action, stop and escalate rather than proceed.
— scope each action's permissions so a failure cannot cascade beyond its task.
— log seeds, inputs, and trajectories so every failure can be replayed and fixed.

attrs.alt: A taxonomy of agent failure classes with the matching mitigation

content.text: Attributing a failure to the correct class is the first step in fixing it, because each class demands a different response. A retry loop around a fatal error wastes budget; exception handling around a silent-wrong output does nothing at all. Mature agents classify failures before reacting to them.
Beyond per-step recovery, system-level guards stop a degraded agent from doing damage or burning budget. A circuit breaker trips after repeated failures, and hard budgets bound total spend and time.
Reliability engineering buys trust and autonomy, but the mechanisms are not free. The table of consequences is worth making explicit before adopting them wholesale.
— recovery turns fatal errors into recoverable ones, and the gain compounds across steps.
— verification and tracing produce a record of what happened and why.
— escalation and fail-closed behavior contain harm instead of amplifying it.
— every verification and retry is an extra call, adding time and money.
— verify/recover/escalate logic is more code to build, test, and maintain.
— over-strict verification can reject correct outputs and trigger needless escalation.
— looping on a bad-auth or malformed-request error wastes budget; classify before retrying.
— a silent error mid-trajectory has already corrupted the result; verify per step.
— retrying a step with side effects can double-charge or duplicate data; make steps idempotent first.
— an unscoped agent failure can cascade beyond its task; bound permissions per action.
— a single green run hides a wide failure distribution; measure over many trajectories.
Verify intermediate results against a specification, not just the absence of exceptions.
Classify failures (transient / fatal / silent / cascading) and respond per class.
Make side-effecting steps idempotent and checkpoint progress so retries and rollback are safe.
Add circuit breakers and hard budgets so a degraded agent halts instead of thrashing.
Report reliability as an end-to-end success-rate distribution with confidence intervals.
a 95%-accurate agent is a reliable agent.
Over a twenty-step task, 95% per-step accuracy compounds to roughly one-in-three end-to-end success; reliability must be measured end to end.
catching exceptions makes an agent robust.
The dangerous failures are silent — plausible wrong outputs that raise no exception; they require verification against a spec, not try/except.
one successful run proves an agent works.
Non-determinism means a single run is weak evidence; reliability is a distribution measured over many trajectories.
Errors compound over steps: per-step reliability p yields roughly p^n end-to-end, so long tasks demand very high per-step reliability.
The most dangerous failures are silent; verification against a specification, not exception handling, is what catches them.
Wrap risky steps in a verify-and-recover envelope so errors are caught where they occur instead of propagating.
Measure reliability as an end-to-end success-rate distribution over many runs, never from a single successful demo.
Idempotency and checkpointing make retries and rollback safe, turning fatal errors into recoverable ones.
